name: Austria
summary: A high-quality, very safe destination — near-free public tuition (~€1,453/yr), immediate compulsory health cover, Vienna's top-ranked livability — and a genuinely open direct skilled-work route (software on the 2026 shortage list, a Red-White-Red Card with no labour-market test, being simplified in 2026). The catch is the citizenship terminus, not work: Austria does NOT permit dual citizenship, so naturalising forfeits BD nationality — the workaround is to hold permanent residence indefinitely and keep the BD passport, treating citizenship as optional. Tempered by a 10-year/B2-German citizenship path, the 4th-highest OECD tax wedge (47.1%) with no Bangladesh treaty, German gating daily life, and the far-right FPÖ's 2024 election win (29.2%).

job-market.summary: Software/data-processing engineers sit on Austria's 2026 nationwide shortage list amid a severe labour shortage (272,000 unfilled roles) — shortage status plus an Austrian degree make a well-prepared graduate genuinely hireable — though the market is Vienna-centric, salaries trail Canada/Germany, and many roles are German-gated.

pros.text: Data-processing/software engineers are on Austria's 2026 nationwide shortage list (expanded to 64 occupations); WKO reports 78% of firms hit by labour shortages and ~272,000 unfilled positions, giving Red-White-Red Card bonus points and faster processing.
Software-engineer pay runs ~€53k–83k (Vienna avg ~€63k–69k) with an AI premium; Vienna alone lists 960+ software-engineering vacancies.

cons.text: The market is heavily Vienna-centric (Graz/Linz secondary) with no multi-hub depth, limiting placement for two earners simultaneously.
Outside Vienna MNCs and startups, German is commonly expected, narrowing roles realistically open to a newly arrived Bangladeshi graduate.

links.title: workinaustria.com — Nationwide shortage occupations (ABA, official agency)

visa-access.summary: Bangladeshi students lodge the D-visa/residence permit through VFS in Dhaka — no travel to Delhi to apply, lower funds proof (~€12,000/yr) than Canada/Australia, and no Bangladesh-specific targeting or ban on stable bilateral ties; the decision is still made by the Austrian Embassy in New Delhi (no decision-making mission in Dhaka) under EU-standard scrutiny.

pros.text: Applications can be lodged at the VFS Austria centre in Dhaka rather than requiring travel to New Delhi; proof of funds (~€12,000/yr) is lower than Canada or Australia.
No Bangladesh-specific visa ban or heightened-scrutiny listing; bilateral ties are normal and a Bangladesh embassy operates in Vienna.

cons.text: The student residence-permit decision is made by the Austrian Embassy in New Delhi (Austria has no decision-making mission in Dhaka), adding a routing step and potential delay.
There is no fast-track student scheme; processing is officially up to ~15 working days but lengthens in the July–September peak.

links.title: BMEIA (Austrian MFA) — Studying in Austria / how to apply (Embassy New Delhi, official)
VFS Global — Austria visa information, Bangladesh

citizenship.summary: The decisive failure for this profile: Austria does not permit dual citizenship, so a naturalising Bangladeshi must renounce BD nationality, and naturalisation requires 10 years' residence plus B2 German — despite a top-tier passport.

pros.text: The Austrian passport ranks joint 4th on the Henley 2026 index with visa-free access to ~185 destinations.

cons.text: Austria does not recognise dual citizenship: standard naturalisation requires renouncing prior nationality (waived only for 'extraordinary achievement', §10(6)), so naturalising would forfeit BD nationality. Workaround: hold Austrian PR (Long-Term Residence-EU, reachable at 5 years) indefinitely and keep the BD passport, treating citizenship as an optional final step.

Standard naturalisation needs 10 years' continuous residence (5 on a permanent permit) plus B2 German (raised from B1 under the 2025–29 government programme), a history/values exam, and 36 months of self-sufficient income in the last 6 years.

post-study-work.summary: Graduates can extend their student permit 12 months to find work, then convert deterministically to a Red-White-Red Card for graduates (no points test, purely offer-based) — a clean, reliable conversion that both spouses can use independently, tempered only by a short runway versus Canada's 3 years.

pros.text: Graduates of Austrian universities may renew the 'Students' permit for 12 months to search for a job or start a business, then apply for a Red-White-Red Card for graduates with no points system — a clear, offer-based conversion.
Both graduating spouses can independently use the 12-month extension and graduate RWR route.

cons.text: The 12-month job-search runway is short next to Canada's 3-year PGWP or Germany's 18 months, compressing the window to land a qualifying offer.
The graduate RWR Card ties the holder to a specific qualifying employer/role initially, limiting work freedom during the transition.

spouse-family.summary: In the work phase an RWR Card Plus grants family members free labour-market access and each spouse can reach their own independent status, with full family reunification and free public schooling; the binding limit is the study phase, where the accompanying spouse of a student faces restricted work rights and combined-funds tests.

pros.text: Family members of a Red-White-Red Card holder can obtain an RWR Card Plus with free labour-market access during the work phase, and if both spouses study each holds their own permit.
Children attend public school free of charge and family reunification is available subject to means and housing.

cons.text: The dependent spouse of a student has restricted work rights during the study phase (own permit/work authorisation needed), and family reunification requires proving higher combined funds.

msc-study.summary: Near-free public tuition (~€1,453/yr for non-EU at TU Wien), 350+ English-taught programmes (32 CS/IT master's) and direct graduate-level admissibility for a BD BSc-CSE make study highly affordable and accessible; the limits are mid-table university rank (QS ~150–200) and bureaucratically restricted non-EU student work.

pros.text: Public-university tuition for non-EU students is only ~€726.72/semester (~€1,453/yr) — near-free — and Austria offers 350+ English-taught programmes, with 32 universities running CS/IT master's (University of Vienna, TU Wien).
A Bangladeshi BSc-CSE is admissible at the graduate level (IELTS/TOEFL), and a standard master's qualifies for the graduate Red-White-Red route.

cons.text: Non-EU students need an employer-applied work permit (Beschäftigungsbewilligung) to work, with a ~20 hrs/week cap and real bureaucracy — far more restrictive than Germany's free allowance.
Austrian universities rank mid-table globally (TU Wien ~QS 197, University of Vienna ~QS 152), below Canadian/UK peers for CS/AI prestige.

links.title: studieren.univie.ac.at — Amount of the tuition fee (University of Vienna, official)

pr-pathway.summary: A clear, sequential chain (RWR Card → RWR Card Plus after 21 months → long-term residence-EU) reaches PR at five years, with shortage-list tech roles comfortably clearing the income and qualification bars; the binding limits are B1 German (Integration Agreement Module 2) and strict absence rules.

pros.text: After 5 years' continuous residence (RWR Card → RWR Card Plus after 21 months of qualifying work) one can obtain a long-term residence–EU permit, removing employer restrictions and annual renewals.
Shortage-list tech roles comfortably meet the income and qualification bars for the RWR sequence.

cons.text: PR requires Module 2 of the Integration Agreement — German at B1 — a real burden for English-first applicants.
Continuity rules are strict (no more than 10 months total, or 6 consecutive months, outside Austria over the 5 years).

links.title: workinaustria.com — Extending your residence permit (ABA, official agency)

income-cost.summary: Vienna's large regulated/social-housing sector keeps rents notably more affordable than Toronto or Sydney, easing the squeeze on a dual-earner household, but a 47% tax wedge eats into two solid-but-not-stellar tech salaries.

pros.text: Vienna's large regulated/social-housing sector keeps rents notably more affordable than other Western capitals, easing the housing squeeze on a dual-earner household.

cons.text: Austria's 47.1% tax wedge (4th-highest OECD) compresses net surplus, and salaries trail Canadian/German levels, so savings potential is moderate rather than strong.
Restricted non-EU student work limits study-phase income, leaning on the ~€12,000/yr funds reserve.

links.title: Study.eu — Austria tuition fees & cost of living (2026)

healthcare.summary: A standout strength: compulsory insurance with no waiting periods and no pre-existing-condition underwriting — employees covered from day one, students self-insure at ~€78.84/month — delivering high-quality e-card/ELGA care and free child co-insurance; the only catch is the student out-of-pocket premium until employment.

pros.text: Compulsory insurance (Pflichtversicherung) has no waiting periods and no pre-existing-condition underwriting: students self-insure with ÖGK at ~€78.84/month and employees are covered from the first day, contributions ~7.65% split with the employer.
High-quality care via the e-card/ELGA system, with children co-insured free with a working parent (to 26 while studying).

cons.text: Student self-insurance is an out-of-pocket monthly cost until employment, and crossing the marginal-earnings threshold shifts a working student onto compulsory employee insurance.

links.title: ÖEAD — Health insurance / Gesundheitskasse (ÖGK), official agency

culture-language.summary: Vienna ranks top globally for livability, but German is essential for daily life and status (B1 for PR, B2 for citizenship), and the social climate for South-Asian Muslim newcomers is cool under a strong far-right.

pros.text: Vienna repeatedly tops global livability and quality-of-life rankings, with excellent public services, transport and work-life balance.
A structured (mandatory) Integration Agreement provides funded German and orientation courses.

cons.text: German is required for daily life and for status (B1 for PR, B2 for citizenship); English works only in Vienna's international workplaces.
Social acceptance of visibly Muslim South-Asian newcomers is mixed amid a strong far-right presence and recurring headscarf/integration debates.

safety-law.summary: Among the safest countries on earth (GPI 2025: 4th) with top-tier rule of law (WJP 2025: 12th, 0.79), tempered by a cool climate toward Muslim migrants under the far-right.

pros.text: Global Peace Index 2025: 4th of 163 (very low violent crime, political neutrality); WJP Rule of Law Index 2025: 12th of 143 (0.79), one of only five EU improvements.
Strong personal and women's safety with reliable, low-corruption civil justice.

cons.text: Anti-Muslim/anti-migrant sentiment is elevated under a strong far-right, with recurring headscarf and integration disputes shaping the lived climate for visible minorities.

links.title: World Justice Project — Rule of Law Index 2025

politics.summary: Institutions are stable (an ÖVP–SPÖ–NEOS coalition formed in 2025), but the far-right FPÖ won the 2024 election outright (29.2%) on a 'Fortress Austria'/'remigration' platform, making the climate hostile to non-EU Muslim migrants.

pros.text: Institutions are stable: despite the FPÖ win, the ÖVP, SPÖ and NEOS formed a three-party coalition in 2025 that excluded the far right, and the shortage-occupation list keeps skilled non-EU recruitment open.

cons.text: The far-right FPÖ won the September 2024 election (29.2%, the largest party) on a 'Fortress Austria' platform calling for 'remigration of uninvited foreigners' and asylum suspension — a powerful anti-immigration force.
The 2025–29 government programme tightened integration/language rules (B1→B2 for citizenship), signalling a restrictive direction.

tax.summary: Among the heaviest tax burdens in the OECD — a 47.1% average wedge (4th-highest) rising to a 58.3% marginal wedge for high earners — with no special expat relief and no Bangladesh tax treaty in force.

pros.text: Tax is assessed individually (no joint penalty on the second earner), and study-phase income is low and lightly taxed.

cons.text: The OECD average tax wedge is 47.1% (4th-highest, 2025), with a 58.3% marginal wedge on high earners and no general expat/inbound-worker relief regime.
There is no Austria–Bangladesh income-tax treaty in force — negotiations were only authorised in July 2024 — so no treaty relief on repatriation.

links.title: OECD — Taxing Wages 2025: Austria country note (PDF)
PwC — Austria: foreign tax relief and tax treaties

community-belonging.summary: Islam is Austria's largest minority religion (8.3%), legally recognised since 1912, with 200+ Vienna mosques and abundant halal — but the Bangladeshi community is small and day-to-day sentiment is strained under the far-right.

pros.text: Islam has been legally recognised in Austria since 1912; Vienna has 200+ mosques and prayer rooms (incl. the Islamic Centre Vienna) and abundant halal food, within an 8.3% Muslim population.
An organised Bangladeshi presence exists in Vienna (embassy, community and sports clubs), easing landing for newcomers.

cons.text: The Bangladeshi community is small relative to Canada/UK, and day-to-day anti-Muslim sentiment is strained under FPÖ rhetoric, headscarf disputes and the 2015 Islam Law's restrictions on foreign mosque funding.

links.title: Embassy & Permanent Mission of Bangladesh, Vienna (official)

direct-work-route.summary: The direct skilled-work entry is the file's clear bright spot — it is the citizenship terminus, not work access, that fails this couple's retain-BD goal. Software/data-processing roles are explicitly on Austria's 2026 nationwide shortage list, where the Red-White-Red Card carries no labour-market test and the Skilled-Worker points bar (55/90) is comfortably cleared by a CS degree + age + English; the EU Blue Card (€55,678 in 2026) is the higher-salary alternative. The RWR Card is applied for from abroad (routed via the Austrian Embassy New Delhi, ~8 weeks, ~€160 fee), the government is actively simplifying it in 2026, and a 2024 Bangladesh–Austria mobility MoU explicitly targets ICT — a genuinely open, low-friction entry. RWR → RWR Plus → Long-Term Residence-EU (PR) at 5 years (B1 German) is clean, but citizenship is 10 years (6 with B2+) AND forces renunciation of BD nationality — so PR-indefinitely is the rational terminus.

pros.text: Software/data roles on the 2026 nationwide shortage list: Red-White-Red Card with no labour-market test, a ~55-point bar a 2022 CS graduate clears easily, ~€160 fee and ~8-week from-abroad processing; the route is being simplified in 2026.
Acute labour shortage (~272,000 vacancies) and a 2024 Bangladesh–Austria mobility MoU explicitly targeting ICT; RWR → PR at 5 years is a clean onward chain.

cons.text: Citizenship requires renouncing BD nationality (Austria bars dual; no fitting exception) and takes 10 years (6 with B2+ German) — the work route is open but the citizenship end-goal fails; PR-indefinitely is the workaround.
Vienna-centric tech market where German is often expected outside MNCs; no decision-making Austrian mission in Dhaka (routed via New Delhi); FPÖ-shaped, family-reunification-restrictive climate.

links.title: Skilled Workers in Shortage Occupations (RWR Card, points grid) — migration.gv.at (official)
